item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one
+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image could not be sourced and the second image's original source could not be accessed, neither with indication they have been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: Double Focus
item.content:encoded:
tl;dr Upvoted. This is now one of my favorite entries in Series One.

I am re-reading this in January of 2017 (my first was over a year ago) and I am really taken with this skip on giving it a second look. This is a lovely article that points, in an understated way, at one of my favorite features of The Foundation: its profoundly callous attitude towards emotional experiences.

What some are reading as caution on the O5's part, I take as heavy-handed and paranoid overreaction to a normal and humane approach to a phenomenon that is easing the pain of many people. There is ambiguity here, though, because there are rational arguments for both sides. I don't understand the previous comments that this ambiguity is confusing or undermines this article. It's very clear what this anomaly does, what kind of threat it poses, and to whom.

Another word for that kind of ambiguity is mystery; the good kind of mystery that challenges the reader and makes it hard to come to an easy answer.

Also this article reminded me of this:

 I died for beauty, but was scarce Adjusted in the tomb, When one who died for truth was lain In an adjoining room. He questioned softly why I failed? "For beauty," I replied. "And I for truth - the two are one; We brethren are," he said. And so, as kinsmen met a night, We talked between the rooms, Until the moss had reached our lips, And covered up our names. --Emily Dickinson

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
timer removed

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 1 week if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ show block
– hide block

I propose a simple edit for this one:

Dr. Carter, principal researcher for SCP-046, is to be removed from his position and reassigned to another project

to

Dr. Carter, principal researcher for SCP-046, is to be removed from his position and reassigned to the SCP-1250 project

There is a Dr. Carter researcher in SCP-1250, and although it doesn't quite have the tragic story context for other edits I've proposed in this vein it does still constitute a valid link, at least in my opinion. It's a fitting article with a continuation of a character seen here. I think showing Foundation scientists being re-assigned is an easy way to do a good link like this, at least some of the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum failed to appear in the app I'm reading on. Scratch that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish there was a way to more discreetly have the original while making it consistently noticeable.
I sincerely doubt O5 would overlook leaving it like this.

Maybe "Level x access required"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, but I don't like it. -1.

Now, to explain, or at least sum up: This feels like an internal self-parody of the site culture wrapped in an SCP report, in that it details a clash between a grim, dark interpretation and a lighter, softer one.

Honestly, if this article dropped the whole "two versions of the same report" schtick and instead went for a tone-neutral feel with the dispute between the researcher and O5 in a memo log behind a collapsible, I'd upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So - it expands a tiny bit every time it eats a body? And they don't mention pruning it in the containment procedures?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like how this took an extremely bland concept and used it to explore the Foundation's tendency to jump to the most insidious conclusions even for relatively benign skips. I actually have a plan in mind for my first skip that is gonna explore that. Solid job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I heard this species is particularly common in those parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This reminds of another SCP I saw on the site…

By the way, you hear about that thing down in Samothrace?

item.title: great job
item.content:encoded: Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Assorted nitpickery:

  • That first photo isn't Polystichum. From what little detail I can see, I'd say probably Pteridium aquilinum (bracken), and even that isn't usually slender and sparse enough to be the fronds depicted.
  • "No anomalous traits have been detected in the molecular composition of the plants." — "Molecular composition" isn't something a biologist would test for — it's hugely vague. "The plants are non-anomalous genetically and metabolically", maybe?
  • "several species of plant matter" — Weird phrasing. "Plant matter from several species", maybe.
  • Why Bermuda grass? It's an invasive weed in the area where this thing lives; wouldn't the skip have incorporated something native instead? I mean, Kentucky bluegrass is totally a thing…
  • "SCP-046 appears to fully decompose individuals exposed to its effects within two hours" — "Appears to"? Why, can't we check for sure?

So yeah, no vote until these are cleared up.

Other than that, though, I actually rather like this one. The meta-story about the internal Foundation disagreements is interesting, which makes up for what might otherwise be a slightly lackluster writeup. If the second writeup was the only thing here, I might downvote for unoriginality; as is, though, it's neat.

item.title: Re: please keep this up
item.content:encoded:
I honestly figured that aside from feeding pattern, it would behave much like any other plant of its type within its environment. It's receiving an equivalent amount of nutrition from humans that it would from photosynthesis and rainfall and it's coexisting within a nonanomalous (I suppose) environment; it's receiving enough energy to survive at its current size, but not quite enough to grow significantly.

I really don't know much about plants, to be honest, but I spent a year living near the plant this is based on, and it never seemed to grow, shrink, thicken, or die off to any noticeable extent.

item.title: please keep this up
item.content:encoded: There is no reason why the foundation would have not found a way to maintain this SCP. Especially after a year of the initial report. In all this time preventative measures could have been taken. I personally love the plant based SCP's and want to know what has happened to it over the past year. Has it been thinned out? Does it have the same affect on people? The growth stages of the plant may cause it to weaken or become more hardy. I have experience with native Kentucky wildflowers and am more than willing to visit this site for follow up research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I really don't know how to react to this one.
I liked it better before the attached document, honestly. I'd have enjoyed it more if there were more implications that it was only previously clasfied as safe due to its mind affecting nature.

Anyway, it's still a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact the two versions are identical is kind of the point to me.

Everyone knows what the SCP does. The difference is one writer goes "The plant gets people to come to it so they can die painlessly, ain't it great, heh feel free to come feed yourself to it, it's the best form of suicide around"; while the other goes "This is a plant that makes people feed themselves to it. Keep the susceptible individuals away".

The facts are the same, it's the interpretation that's different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing is, for all they know the plant may induce suicidal tendencies, or cause illnesses, in order to garner more food. They don't want to go "This might be a mercy kill" because, well, the foundation isn't exactly a bastion of mental stability, and it could easily end up feeding, going worldwide, and causing mass death.

They can't risk telling people "it might be pleasant" because EVEN IF IT IS PLEASANT, it's still massively dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Version 1 is based on presumption of compulsion, version 2 is based on presumption of willing victims only. Both interpretations are equally valid within the data provided by the article, but the Foundation is full of scientists, and they would not leave such an important ambiquity alone. The test is simple: find a terminally ill person who is "raging against the dying of the light", as it were. If a terminal patient who still very much wants to live until the last second possible (and there are no shortage of them in the world's cancer wards), suddenly becomes resigned to their fate and wants to pass on peacefully; well then we know that 46 exerts a compulsion and version 1 is correct. If patient X retains his/her "spit in death's eye" attitude until they get that chance, never gettign the urge to have a nice quiet lay-down in the woods, then we know there is no compulsion and version 2 is correct.

Still, I like the idea here. A contrast between competing philosophies within the organization. Well written, to boot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost seems weird to find an article with no [DATA EXPUNGED], [DATA REDACTED] or black boxes. Not that that's a bad thing, just seems like standard procedure to black out the name of the location at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: sweet retelling

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't save you, except from a life of chronic pain and suffering…

The effects are generally exactly the same in both versions but described using a different viewpoint. The article could be viewed as a commentary on euthanasia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is well written and weird in a good way. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a thing what draws you in to kill you… except it actually saves you… except it actually kills you… except it actually saves you… except don't listen to that idiot it totally kills you… I just can not get into that kind of back and forth. If you're going to set up a cognitive dissonance, I feel you should do it in a clear way; this just confuses me, and not in an artistic or enjoyable way. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a plant what dissolves animals.

It doesn't…act unexpectedly.

This and SCP-1087 are the times when I realize that most of the membership of the wiki and I have somewhat or radically different opinions in what "weird" is. I mean, feel free to downvote if it doesn't grab you, but it always seemed personally strange to me that "plant that euthanizes you for food" would seem boring to people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I also just realized I left something out; the Foundation would be keeping it from the public, obviously, my point was that they wouldn't be explicitly lying to their own staff just for the sake of it. They would say "We've got this thing here that might be a sweet, innocent, Mercy Kill Plant… But we're not sure, so for fuck's sake people don't happily lay down in front of it and wait for the vines to digest you." Only, y'know, more profesionally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ambiguity of which version is the "true" one is nice, definitely better than the "that was the FALSE version of the report, the one we show to idiots, and here's now the real thing" thing that some articles do for no real reason. However, in order for the ambiguity to work three things must be true:

  1. The ambiguity must be ambiguous. People should be genially unable to decide which version of the article is the true one, no matter how hard they think or how many times they reread the article. 046 does this part reasonably well.
  2. The competing versions must be different enough. Making two versions of SCP-076, one of which describes Able as wearing a red shirt, and another - a blue shirt, wouldn't work, because I don't care at all what kind of shirt Able wears. In case of 046, the plant thing acts more or less the same in both versions - it attracts prey, then dissolves it. The only difference is whether or not the prey is willing. Maybe for some people that's enough of a difference, I don't know, but for me both versions are pretty much identical.
  3. At least one version, preferably both, must be interesting enough on its own. That is the point where 046 fails the most for me. It's a plant what dissolves animals. It doesn't look very weird, doesn't act unexpectedly, doesn't have some secret agenda or old history with sinister implications. It's just boring. And juggling several boring things isn't very interesting, no matter how impressively you juggle. That's why they put those things on fire at circus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: o_O Really? That's EXACTLY what I consider "the world is not ready". It's currently being contained and the general public is held at bay because we don't know that this thing only needs 14 more people to get eaten before SHRUBZILLA, DESTROYER OF WORLDS is unleashed. I never said that it wouldn't still be researched, just that it would be sequestered from public knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, either I fail at writing or you at reading.

Could always be a little of both! :3

I get where you guys are coming from now, yes. It's quite likely that the Foundation would try to come to such an understanding of a helpful SCP that it could get released to the world as an -EX, in my mind there would be a strong push for this in the organization. There would also be a very sterile bureaucratic force that would just push to smother, hide and contain.

Ultimately though, if something was good but also too anomalous to be ever explained within our understanding of science it would be buried forever, and probably not even harnessed due to the inherent paranoia of Foundation. Secret conspiracy metagovernmental organizations are not benevolent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But that wouldn't stop them from using it or trying to learn from it. If the Foundation was just concerned about stopping the items from doing anything, they wouldn't do all this research. There's a reason the Foundation doesn't just destroy everything anomalous they get their hands on like the GOC does. If the Foundation could reverse engineer 500 they would, and they'd probably do their best to eventually make it something available to the public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Um, either I fail at writing or you at reading.
Of course they'd contain it, but the reason for it is that it's an anomaly which we don't understand and as such has a massive fuckup potential. That's not what I consider standard The World Is Not Ready reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What? No! The Foundation would absolutely contain a beneficial anomalous object purely because it was anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it's 3… No. The Foundation has a LOT of stuff going on that could be "beneficial" but we keep it locked away for the standard The World Is Not Ready reasons.

Seconding Anaxagoras on this being bullshit.
What Foundation actually does is prevent kittens licking antifreeze because it tastes nice.
To understand - it's usually a bad idea to use something we don't understand, to the extent some SCPs can be used. Never mind that most of them, there isn't even sufficient amounts of them for their usage to have much point or impact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the original one as a dangerous/scary SCP. Then i kind of frowned when i saw that there was a second safe article, but then upon reading the discussion and the possible reasoning/unknowns behind it, i +1'ed with the fury of 1000 suns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the case of three, if it really is totally benign, the Foundation probably wouldn't be utterly opposed to anyone ever using it ever. Remember, the Foundation doesn't just research things to understand them for the sake of understanding them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sort of on the fence about this, mostly because of the REASONING between the different write-ups. I can really only think of three reasons: 1) the doc was under some sort of mental or memetic compulsion from the SCP; 2) the doc thought it was "beneficial" and figured that writing up something relatively passive and genteel would maybe make folks think it was okay to use as a euthanasia plant; or 3) the Foundation wants to make sure nobody thinks it's a good idea to get around this thing willingly.

If it's 1, that's a good hook, emphasize it more because there's nothing really that shows even knowing ABOUT this thing is memetic, but if you're actively around it and it's a cognitohazard, THAT's something.
If it's 2, that's not as good a hook IMO, but could make for an interesting tale.
If it's 3… No. The Foundation has a LOT of stuff going on that could be "beneficial" but we keep it locked away for the standard The World Is Not Ready reasons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those of you who otherwise have concerns or criticisms, I'm open to suggestions on how to improve the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, because the US government would have a hard time doing that, and they wield a fair bit more power for that kind of thing than the Foundation does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, 50km isn't the containment zone. It's just saying that nobody outside of that area has ever been affected by 046. That's more the "look and see if any people with terminal illnesses start planning day trips that they don't plan on coming back from" zone.

Now I'm imagining what the parking lot nearest to 046 looks like…

EDIT: Okay, I saw that that wasn't explained as well. Added a line to clarify the 50km radius part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's still risk in not precisely defining what this thing does. I need to know how safe it is to stand next to this thing, because if I'm being chased by a bear, I need to know what is and what is not more dangerous.

The ambiguity is cute, but really doesn't add much. The differences between the two are rather more than tonal, so there is certainly a truth to this. Also, see the comment I edited in about size of the containment zone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's more the attitude the Foundation displays towards the SCP than towards people approaching it. The original researcher believed the entity had an essentially beneficial nature, and wrote a containment document that reflected that. The rewritten version presumes that the object is dangerous and writes a document accordingly. The issue is that between the two versions, you can't know what the actual object does; the article is (in addition to presenting the SCP) intended to show the level of discretion that different researchers or policymakers have in how containment is handled. Moreover, it's possible that the first version is actually correct and the second version was written by someone with mental instability brought on by exposure to the SCP.

Also, neither version really imperils personnel; the containment procedures are basically "get away from this if you want to live." The first version just makes that a lot stricter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This doesn't work for me. There is literally no reason the Foundation would lie about something like this. There's lots of SCPs that are perfectly safe to be around. If the Foundation doesn't want this thing used by dying Foundation personnel, then they just forbid that. Lying about the object's properties serves no real purpose, and actually imperils personnel by giving them incomplete relevant information.

Also, a 50km radius is gigantic, especially in a relatively densely populated area like the US. You can't just make a region that big disappear. That's almost four times larger than the largest county in Kentucky. That's pretty ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, The idea behind it is compelling. This should be used as an alternative to cremation upon death. I'd much rather go in a state of euphoria.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a lot like something I had rotting in my sandbox for quite the time, except you manage to , well, not make it boring as fuck.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoop, good catch. That's always the step I miss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, I just noticed: you still need to put the title in the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, admittedly it's the diptych that really sells it, it probably wouldn't survive as a single SCP report, but I really like the way this outlines the attitudes of people working around such weird stuff all the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have actually come very close to writing SCPs based on ideas I got from Xanth books, but I only read the one and it was a long time ago. This isn't it; it's actually based on a giant-ass bush we have in our backyard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems really, really reminiscent of Tangle Trees from the Xanth books. So much so that I can't really see it as anything else. It's quite well written, though, and I like the ambiguity in which version is correct… I'm going to abstain from voting at this time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those that I wrote a while back and left in my sandbox. But it was one that got some better responses, so I dusted it off, tweaked it up a bit, and posted it.

The idea here is that you can't know which version is more accurate. The researcher in the second one, Dr. Carter, is supposed to be a generally jaded sort of fellow, used to examining dark eldritch horrors all day, and he's excited to find something that's anomalous but…pleasant. But the Foundation isn't in the practice of just accepting the idea that an SCP can be beneficial (since they usually aren't, even when they seem to be). So I leave it up to you to decide which version you think is the truth.
